Singapore rower Saiyidah Aisyah scribbles words in her journal, a sort of raw accounting of her days.

ST PHOTO: MARK CHEONG

Rohit Brijnath

Published脗Jul 27, 2024, 11:04 PM

Updated脗Jul 28, 2024, 01:21 AM

PARIS芒聙聯The name of a stranger from a foreign land is written on a sticker in Jacob Plihal芒聙聶s boat.

芒聙聹Kim,芒聙聺 it says.

She isn芒聙聶t Plihal芒聙聶s friend, she isn芒聙聶t family. This polite, 2.08m American hasn芒聙聶t even ever met the Australian. But under a gently pecking rain at the Vaires-sur-Marne Nautical Stadium in Paris, as we discuss motivation, this 28-year-old with an architectural degree mentions her name.

芒聙聹I芒聙聶ll put little stickers in my boat sometimes,芒聙聺 explains Plihal. 芒聙聹Like a little mantra that芒聙聶s been impactful lately. So one that comes to mind is just a name, Kim Brennan.

芒聙聹She was an Australian rower, single sculler, very successful, won (gold at) the 2016 Olympics. And I think she芒聙聶s a very good influence on technique. So just writing 芒聙聹Kim芒聙聺 in my boat, I see that and I visualise how she rows and that芒聙聶s someone I try to model (myself on).芒聙聺

These Games are fiercer than we can imagine and races unfold like hasty, unforgiving interrogations. Just to make it clear, the Italian fencer Luigi Samele put it bluntly: 芒聙聹The goal of the Olympics, of course, is not to have a nice walk around the Olympic Village, but to go home with medals芒聙聺.

But in this festival committed to the athletic deeds, don芒聙聶t underestimate the power of words. Coaches in Paris shout them. Psychologists use them as balm. Athletes mutter them in call rooms. And sometimes, as with former medal-winning gymnast Louis Smith, they even tattoo reminders onto their arms: 芒聙聹What I Deserve I Earn芒聙聺.

Singapore rower Saiyidah Aisyah scribbles words in her journal, a sort of raw accounting of her days. Words to rise to and go to sleep with. Words to wear like armour. Words that before Rio 2016 芒聙聯 the first Olympics she qualified for 芒聙聯 she pasted to her wall almost in the thousands. Some were truths, some a plea, some an athletic prayer.

芒聙聹Train to be bulletproof芒聙聺.

芒聙聹It is meant to be hard芒聙聺.

芒聙聹I am *&^%^& amazing.芒聙聺

This year, making a comeback after stepping away from the sport, Aisyah turned to words again. Filled her journal with scribbles as if writing them down was reassurance. Just before the Games she shared two pages of them with The Straits Times.

芒聙聹I am strong, lifting heavy at the gym, 11 on the chin ups. Powerful.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I have done this before. Trust. Let go.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I know what pain feels like. I feel it every day out there on water. This is nothing different.芒聙聺

Incredibly after barely a year back, Aisyah qualified for Paris but on July 27, under a sulky grey sky, she couldn芒聙聶t find her best self. In Rio, in her quarter-finals, she timed 7min 56sec across 2,000m; in Paris,in the heats where she came fifth out of five competitors, she stopped the clock at 8:17.04.

芒聙聹I know what I can do,芒聙聺 she said later, being unsparing with her words 芒聙聹and I did not do it today. The power was just not there. The speed was not there. Rowing is simple. It芒聙聶s just put the blades in and take the blades out. And sometimes you may make it more complicated.芒聙聺

Rowers embody strength, as if they芒聙聶re massive human engines fitted neatly into slim boats. But these gleaming bodies, these flat, competitive faces, are partly lies. In sport, toughness always resides beside insecurity. Behind everyone芒聙聶s fibre, doubt rustles.

芒聙聹(I芒聙聶m) definitely trying to keep strong, positive thoughts,芒聙聺 says Olympic first-timer Paige Badenhorst of South Africa. 芒聙聹Acknowledging the demons that, yes, I芒聙聶m nervous, everyone else is nervous. But also reminding myself of my strengths, what you do have that will help you fight those demons.芒聙聺

To ward off these demons is to reach for words. To remind themselves of who they are. What they芒聙聶ve done. How they芒聙聶ve earned their way here. And so Badenhorst, part of the 2022 Cambridge crew who won the annual Boat Race on the River Thames, has words echoing in her head.

芒聙聹Calm. Cool. Collected.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Fierce. Ruthless.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Just repeat those words to myself.芒聙聺

During a race?

芒聙聹Before, during, after,芒聙聺 she says with laugh.

And it芒聙聶s not just her, it芒聙聶s champions who reach for words to achieve that perfect state they芒聙聶re searching for. Emma Twigg, 37, is the defending Olympic champion in the women芒聙聶s single sculls, has a licence to skipper boats, can repair cycles and has an academic degree in sports. Still for all this, for all her four Games before Paris, she needs to talk to herself at the start.

芒聙聹You芒聙聶ve done this a million times.芒聙聺

Twigg is surging forward, Aisyah has one more chance on July 28 in the repechage. 芒聙聹It芒聙聶s me just against myself and whatever is in my head,芒聙聺 she said a little mournfully. There芒聙聶s no guarantee in sport, but there is one thing you can be sure she did after her race.

She went back to the Village. Pulled out her journal. And found some refuge in words.
